coredata.dc:title: Effect of carbon quantum dots doping on structural, optical, and antibacterial properties of barium tungstate nanocomposite material
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of King Saud University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of King Saud University.
coredata.dc:creator: Aslam, Nouman
Akram, Saba
Yousaf, M.I.
Alshammari, Abdulrahman
Albekairi, Norah A.
Fu, D.J.
coredata.dc:description: Local bacterial infection remains an increasingly severe threat to human health worldwide, and infection control is still a challenging task. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQSs) and barium tungstate show antibacterial properties. CQDs doped Barium Tungstate (BaWO4) are synthesized by using a hydrothermal route and their optical and antimicrobial activities against the Gram-positive bacteria staphylococcus aureus and optical properties were investigated. The UV–Vis reveals a shift in the bandgap from 3.62 eV to 2.93 eV due to doping of CQDs in BaWO4. The structure of the CQDs/BaWO4 were studied by XRD, which shows CQDs doped BaWO4 samples possess tetragona4 werelite structure with the preferred orientation of (112) identified by XRD at 26° having crystallite size ∼ 31 nm. Nanocomposite BaWO4/CQDs exhibit a spherical-like shape and are composed of Ba, O, W and C according to the design of the experiment. Zeta sizer of CQDs by DLS shows the size of CQDs is 7.65 nm. The FT-IR shows the presence of functional groups in doped material like CC, CO, and COOH bonds which indicate that the C-dots are functionalized with epoxy, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxylic acid groups. Their elemental composition and surface morphology were studied by EDS which shows the percentage variation of CQDs in BaWO4, SEM results show the change in shape of the sample by adding CQDs to BaWO4. The TEM image proves the presence of doped material in the BaWO4. The PL spectra reveals the emission spectra shift towards a higher wavelength and absorption increases. The qualitative analysis shows the antimicrobial activity and enlargement of the inhibition zone and quantitative analysis confirms it.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Facile synthesis
BaWO4
CQDs
Optical properties
Antimicrobial properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Stepwise fabrication of CQDs, (b) The Zeta sizer of CQDs.
The stepwise synthesis. (a) BaWO4. (b) CQDs doped BaWO4.
FTIR spectrum of CQDs and BaWO4 and its composites.
XRD pattern of BaWO4 and BaWO4/CQDs composites.
The SEM image of (A) BaWO4 and its doped composites with (B) 3 mL, (C) 6 mL, and (D) 8 mL CQDs.
EDS spot with (a) graph and (b) SEM image with spot.
TEM of 6 ml CQDs doped BaWO4 with highlighted doped CQDs.
Absorbance of BaWO4 and its composites with 3 mL, 6 mL and 8 mL CQDs and bare CQD s.
The bandgap of BaWO4 and its composites with 3 mL, 6 mL and 8 mL CQDs and bare CQD s.
The PL spectra of (A) BaWO4, (B) BaWO4 doped with 3 ml CQDs, (C) 6 ml CQDs, (D) 8 ml CQDs, (E) CQDs.
The inhibition zone (A) BaWO4, (B) BaWO4 doped with 3 mL CQDs (C) BaWO4 doped with 6 mL CQDs (D) BaWO4 doped with 8 mL CQDs (E).
The antibacterial activity of BaWO4 (A) and its composites with 3 mL (B), 6 mL (C) and 8 mL (D) CQDs and pure CQDs (E).
Inoculated and surviving bacterial colonies at 0 h and 24 h of BaWO4 (A) and its composites with 6 mL CQDs (C) against S. Aureus bacteria.
Antimicrobial activity of pure BaWO4, CQDs, and the composites of CQDs doped BaWO4.

head.ce:title: Effect of carbon quantum dots doping on structural, optical, and antibacterial properties of barium tungstate nanocomposite material

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Sciences, National Textile University, Faisalabad, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Sciences, National Textile University, Faisalabad, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Post Box 2455, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Artificial Micro- and Nano-Materials of Ministry of Education and Hubei Nuclear Solid Physics Key Laboratory, School of Physics and Technology, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Artificial Micro- and Nano-Materials of Ministry of Education and Hubei Nuclear Solid Physics Key Laboratory

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Artificial Micro- and Nano-Materials of Ministry of Education and Hubei Nuclear Solid Physics Key Laboratory, School of Physics and Technology, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Local bacterial infection remains an increasingly severe threat to human health worldwide, and infection control is still a challenging task. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQSs) and barium tungstate show antibacterial properties. CQDs doped Barium Tungstate (BaWO4) are synthesized by using a hydrothermal route and their optical and antimicrobial activities against the Gram-positive bacteria staphylococcus aureus and optical properties were investigated. The UV–Vis reveals a shift in the bandgap from 3.62 eV to 2.93 eV due to doping of CQDs in BaWO4. The structure of the CQDs/BaWO4 were studied by XRD, which shows CQDs doped BaWO4 samples possess tetragona4 werelite structure with the preferred orientation of (112) identified by XRD at 26° having crystallite size ∼ 31 nm. Nanocomposite BaWO4/CQDs exhibit a spherical-like shape and are composed of Ba, O, W and C according to the design of the experiment. Zeta sizer of CQDs by DLS shows the size of CQDs is 7.65 nm. The FT-IR shows the presence of functional groups in doped material like CC, CO, and COOH bonds which indicate that the C-dots are functionalized with epoxy, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxylic acid groups. Their elemental composition and surface morphology were studied by EDS which shows the percentage variation of CQDs in BaWO4, SEM results show the change in shape of the sample by adding CQDs to BaWO4. The TEM image proves the presence of doped material in the BaWO4. The PL spectra reveals the emission spectra shift towards a higher wavelength and absorption increases. The qualitative analysis shows the antimicrobial activity and enlargement of the inhibition zone and quantitative analysis confirms it.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibacterial agents have significant importance in different industries such as textiles, construction, water disinfection, food, packaging, and medicine. Antibiotic-resistant bacteria and pathogens have emerged as a severe and growing public health concern because of the extensive use of antibiotics. There have been great innovations to enhance the antibacterial properties of materials because nanoparticles prove to be good against bacterial infections. These particles directly attack the cell wall of bacteria, rather than the antibiotics they penetrate the drugs also. So, the attention of researchers moves towards nanoparticles due to their less resistant behavior of nanoparticles against drugs rather than antibiotics which shows the resistant behavior against the drugs [1,2]. Several nanomaterials are used for antibacterial properties such as zinc, titanium, graphene, magnesium, etc. but carbon quantum dots and barium tungstate have gained much attention.

Recent studies reveal that BaWO4 shows antibacterial properties which was done by P. Nivetha et al. found the anti-microbial properties of the BaWO4/MoS2 against the Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria which reveals their inhibition activity [2,3]. The CQDs also show the applications like drug delivery, photocatalysis, photodynamic therapy, electrocatalysis, biosensing, and chemical sensing [2]. The previous work on CQDs is also shows that CQDs possess antibacterial properties [3]. From the alkaline earth tungstate, Barium Tungstate BaWO4 is the heaviest one. Like other compounds, BaWO4 can also take the shape according to the conditions present in the surrounding which can mostly give the shape of tetragonal scheelite [4]. While the CQDs show shape of the flake-like structure in the nanometers or sub-nanometric thickness [5]. Such types of dimensions of the CQDs can affect the optical response, their quantum yield, and their color. The CQDs show good photoluminescent properties [6]. BaWO4 has great properties like good electrical conductivity, photoluminescence properties, mechanical properties like the light-emitting diodes, sensors that can sense humidity, formaldehyde detector, optical filters, scintillator detectors, photocatalysis, dielectric which can use microwaves, phosphors and they can be used in solid-state lasers [7].

BaWO4 has so many applications due to its wide efficiency in the degradation of natural contaminates. While BaWO4 is also a very good photocatalyst, but it has one main issues: Its behavior under UV light is dynamic [8]. Under UV light electron-hole matches occur due to that BaWO4 is unstable [9]. The steps were being taken by scientists to overcome this issue by the controlled structure of the BaWO4 with which they can make it able to perform well for degradation of the dyes present in the industrial effluents [10]. Due to these limitations, steps were being taken to resolve these issues by doping this with other materials like metals (or) nonmetals, co-doping with other semiconductors can change their photocatalytic properties, and also improve their inhibition activity [11]. When the surface change strategies were taken, the mixing of two semiconductors with different bandgaps can perform in an improved way through photo-generated electron-hole separation [12].

Due to this CQDs were chosen for doping with BaWO4 because they are inexpensive materials and they can add marvelous properties when doped with other materials [13]. The functional groups present in CQD hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups increase the CQD reactivity and their hydrophilicity. These functional groups present on them can make them the best option to combine with the BaWO4.The CQDs are used in traceable targeted delivery and several therapy applications due to their very small size [14]. The CQDs doped materials can also serve for bio-imaging and photoelectronic applications [15].

The BaWO4 is dynamic in nature due to small band gap so, immediate recombination and disappearance of electron-hole matches in BaWO4 under UV Light is produced. This can decrease the photocatalytic properties. The mixing of two semiconductors heaving different band gaps can increase the photocatalytic behavior by proficient photogenerated electron hole separation. While studying the previous literature we come to a point that there is no work done in the synthesizing of the CQDs doped BaWO4 nanocomposite Because BaWO4 with MoS2 gives. The immediate recombination and disappearance of electron-hole matches in BaWO4 under UV Light, can decrease the photocatalytic properties. The mixing of two semiconductors heaving different band gaps can increase the photocatalytic behavior by proficient photogenerated electron hole separation controlled structure of BaWO4 by hydrothermal route lessen the successful degradation of bacteria and give a small inhibition zone [2]. Our main objective is to synthesize the CQDs doped BaWO4 by hydrothermal route. Fabrication of nano-composite CQDs/BaWO4 changes the surface strategies, these two nanocomposites with different bandgaps can significantly improve the electron-hole separation, to increase the inhibition zone, anti-bacterial, and sensing properties.

The chemicals sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O) and barium nitrate Ba(NO3)2 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich company. Ethanol is used for purification, distilled water as a solvent, and Ascorbic Acid purchased from Merck company with 99 % purity. 2.5 Molar solution of sodium hydroxide to maintain the pH of the solution at 9.

CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal route. In their preparation 30 mL distilled water was taken into a beaker and then added 20 mL ethanol into it. After their mixing 2.5 g of the ascorbic acid was put into the solution and then placed on the magnetic stirrer for about 20 min at 900 rpm to make a clear solution. This as-prepared mixture was then poured into the Teflon-lined autoclave of 200 mL and put this autoclave into the oven at 120 °C for 120 min. After that when the time was completed and the cooling down of the autoclave naturally. The sample was then filtered with a 0.2 μm filter. Stepwise synthesis of CQDs is represented by Fig. 1 (a).

Two solutions were prepared for the fabrication of the BaWO4. In solution A; 8 g barium nitrate Ba(NO3)2 was dissolved in an aqueous solution that contains 28 mL of distilled water and 12 mL acetic acid (CH3COOH) which was stirred well to make the clear solution. In solution B; 1.76 g of sodium tungstate dihydrate Na2WO4·2H2O dissolved in 40 mL of distilled water to make solution B as shown in Fig. 2 (a).

At the same solution B was added to A, drop by drop under constant magnetic stirring for 2 h and at the same time, 2.5 M NaOH solution was used to maintain the pH of the solution to about 9. After the mixing of the solutions, it was transferred to the autoclave of 200 mL for about 20 h and 170 °C. Then the obtained suspension was filtered and wash it by using distilled water to neutralize the obtained nanoparticles to remove the NaOH until the pH of the precipitates reaches (≈7) and the last wash was performed with ethyl alcohol to remove all kinds of impurities. The obtained nanoparticles were then dried at 60 °C for 8 h in the oven. Then pure BaWO4 is formed which is demonstrated as sample “A”. The stepwise illustration is shown in Fig. 2 (a).

The synthesis procedure for CQDs doped BaWO4 was the same as BaWO4, but the only difference was that CQDs were added in solution B. In this way, 3 samples were prepared by using the CQDs with different amounts 3 mL as sample “B”, 6 mL as sample “C”, 8 mL as sample “D” and pure CQDs are demonstrated as sample “E” The stepwise synthesis is shown in Fig. 2(b).

EQUINOX 3000 X-ray diffraction (XRD), Thermo-scientific, France with Cu kα (λ = 1.54 Å) was used to investigate the crystal structure. Surface morphology and elemental composition of sample CQDs/BaWO4 were studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS)–MAIA3. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) JEM-2100F; JEOL Ltd. Japan was used to study particle size. Photocatalytic behavior was studied with Shimadzu LAMBDA 950 UV–Vis DRS in the range of 200–800 nm. By using a Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer-Spectrum Two-FTIR) functional groups were studied which confirms the formation of CQDs doped BaWO4. The Zeta sizer of the CQDs was calculated by using the Malvern Zeta-sizer instrument (Malvern, Zeta-sizer Nano, UK).

The antibacterial property of BaWO4, CQDs, and BaWO4/CQDs nanocomposite was studied using the agar well diffusion technique. The Gram (+ve) (Staphylococcus aureus) microbiological strain was used for the study. The solutions of (BaWO4, CQDs, and CQDs/BaWO4) were made by using DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide) and their amounts were set at 200 mg/mL, with Amikacin and Nystatin serving as antibacterial and reference medicine, respectively. The antimicrobial discs with (Samples 200 mg/ mL on Whatman No. 1. filter disc) were put on the agar plate after a sterile cotton swab created a lawn of test organism on the agar plate. The incubation of plates was done at 37 °C for 24 h. The zones were calculated in millimeters.

Quantitative analysis for antibacterial was performed to evaluate the antibacterial activity of composite against S. aureus by using the 20743:2013 Transfer Method. Samples were placed on agar plates with 1 ml of bacterial culture seeded into them, and samples were then compressed for 60 s using a 200 g cylindrical weight. Samples were taken from the agar surface after 60 s and placed inverted on an empty petri plate for 18 h at 37 °C. Following a 24-hour incubation period, the samples were immersed in 20 ml of saline solution, vortexed for 1 min, and then serially diluted to 104. Agar plates were prepared first, and then 60 µl of each dilution was dispersed throughout the surface before all plates were incubated. The identical procedure was used again at zero hour, however after the bacteria had attached themselves to the composite, incubation was not carried out. Then the antibacterial activity and percentage decrease based on the number of surviving bacterial colonies for samples (A & C) at 0 h and 24 h were calculated.

The size distribution of CQDs was calculated by Zeta sizer and the result is shown in Fig. 1(b). There is the visible peak which demonstrates their average particle size is 7.65 nm. The size of CQDs was calculated using the dynamic light scattering distribution (DLS) by making the dispersed solution of CQDs in the distilled water. The hydrodynamic size is normally greater than the size calculated in vacuum. The functional groups present in CQDs make them ease of dispersion in water-based solvents. The as-synthesized CQDs by the hydrothermal route are green photo-luminescent particles as shown in Fig. 1(a). The hydrodynamic size of the CQDs which is less than previously calculated by Sarita Devi et al in 2018 [16].

FTIR spectrum is taken 500–4000 cm−1. In Fig. 3 shows FTIR spectra of CQDs, BaWO4, and the composites of BaWO4 doped with CQDs (B, C, D). The FTIR results of CQDs show the presence of the CC, CO, and COOH bonds which indicate that the C-dots are functionalized with epoxy, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxylic acid groups [17]. The absorption band of the OH is located at 3500 cm−1. The stretching vibrations at 1700 cm−1 confirm the presence of CO and the CO bonding shown at 1100 cm−1 and 870 cm−1 indicates the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups. OH vibrations are also given a broad absorption of 3000–3500 cm−1 and CO shows a strong peak at 1550 cm−1. Furthermore, CH, CC, and CC related to three distinct absorption peaks due to stretching vibrations at 2950 cm−1, 1420 cm−1, and 1330 cm−1, implying the existence of alkyl and aryl groups. Meanwhile, hydrophilic groups (COC, CO, OH, CO) allow CQDs to diffuse readily in water and other polar solvents. The CQDs possess high hydrophilicity making them ideal for functionalization for detection and diagnostic applications [18]. The presence of these functional groups imparts the hydrophilic properties in the CQDs without doing any more modifications to the CQDs.

The band of as-synthesized BaWO4 present at position ∼1380 cm−1 reveals the stretching and bending vibrations of the groups like CH2 and OH. The band present at ∼824 cm−1 shows the strong antisymmetric stretching vibrations of WO in the tetrahedron like WO4 2− which was reported previously, verifying the formation of the BaWO4 nanoparticles [19]. In these graphs, the transmittance of CQDs in the barium tungstate is seen, indicating that the functional groups of CQDs like CO -C and CH2 are introduced in the barium tungstate [20]. These introductions of the new bands like COC at 870 cm−1, CO at 1700 cm−1 and CO at 1100 cm−1 in the BaWO4 can verify the formation of the nanocomposite with different concentrations of CQDs (B, C, D).

The phases of BaWO4 and BaWO4/CQDs composites were studied by XRD, and their crystallite sizes were calculated as shown in Fig. 4 . BaWO4 has a visible peek at 23.9° in samples doped with different amounts (B, C, D) of carbon quantum dots. The diffraction peaks of BaWO4 are (112), (004), (202), (204), (220), (116), (312), (224), (400), (208), (316), (332), and (404), located, respectively, at 2θ angles of 26.448°, 31.859°, 42.946°, 45.676°, 48.65°, 53.55°, confirming the formation of the tetragonal scheelite structure of BaWO4 (Reference code: 01-085-0588) [21]. The peaks for CQDs in BaWO4 can be verified by the peaks present at 22°, 26.42°, 42.36°, 54.12°, and 77.40° (Reference code: 00-008-0415), confirming the presence of the CQDs. The shift of diffraction peaks is due to a decrease in strain lagging and d-spacing by the incorporation of the CQDs. The crystallinity size of the BaWO4 was calculated with the help of the Debye Scherrer formula which is seen in equation (1) [22].
(1)
D = k λ / β C o s Θ

In the above equation, λ is the wavelength that was used by the XRD machine which has the Cu-kα radiation of about 0.154 nm and β is the full-width half maxima (FWHM) of the peak. By using the above equation, we can calculate the crystal size of all synthesized nanoparticles. The diffraction peak present at (002) is the confirmation of the CQDs which was seen by Q. H. Liang et al. in 2013 [18]. The crystal size of BaWO4 (112) and the composites as doped with 3 mL, 6 mL, and 8 mL CQDs are 34 nm, 31.59 nm, 34.3 nm, and 31.74 nm, respectively.

The morphology of BaWO4 nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 5 which confirms the formation of the nanoparticles. The composite particles of CQDs/BaWO4 5(B, C, D) are semi-rod-like structures and their size is in the nm range. The particles in Fig. 5 are seen as a change in their shape from rod-like to flower-like in Fig. 5(C) 6 mL CQDs and then they become spherical in Fig. 5(D) 8 ml CQDs. That is the reason why they can be able to apply for various applications like sensing, energy conversion, and photocatalytic treatment [20].

The difference in size compared with the previous literature is due to the diverse synthesizing technique. In Fig. 5(A) the particles of BaWO4 have tetragonal scheelite structure. In Fig. 5(B) sample shows the shape of the crystals being changed slightly due to the presence of CQDs. In Fig. 5(C) sample is slightly becoming more compressed and in D sample in Fig. 5(D) the shape is getting more dispersed due to the agglomeration occurring in D (8 mL of CQDs) concentration.

Fig. 6 (a) shows elemental composition of the barium tungstate as it was doped with CQDs. The carbon peak is visible in the graph and the arrow allocates the point from where the EDS was done Fig. 6 (b). All the elements are shown in the Table 1 , which shows the elemental composition by weight percent of each element present in the composite. The peaks of barium and oxygen and tungstate are seen clearly with the weight percentage of carbon which varies as 0.31 %, 6.21 %, 5.87 % and weight percentage of oxygen is 12.06 %, and for barium is 40.82 % and the weight percentage of tungstate is 39.94 % as seen in the graphs and also in Table 1. Fig. 6 and Table 1 confirms the CQDs doped barium tungstate is produced. The percentage of CQDs and Ba, W and O is also present in Table 1.

The chemical structure and morphology of CQDs doped BaWO4 were investigated by several characterization methods, uniformly distributed as shown in Fig. 7 . The transmission electron microscope is a very effective device in material science. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of the sample shows that these carbon dots are separated from each other and have a homogeneous dispersion without evident aggregation and a relatively narrow size distribution. To prepare the samples for detection under TEM, approximately 4 µL of sample suspension was dropped on the TEM carbon grid. The TEM results of 6 ml CQDs doped BaWO4 shows that the incorporation of CQDs into the BaWO4 structure is produced and CQDs are present homogeneously in the entire structure due to which their photocatalytic properties are enhanced, and antibacterial properties are also increased. The doped CQDs are highlighted in Fig. 7.

The UV–Vis of the pure BaWO4 and its composites with CQDs of 3 mL, 6 mL, 8 mL were recorded, which are seen in Fig. 8 . The value of absorption increases as we doped the BaWO4 with 3 mL, 6 mL and 8 mL of CQDs this phenomenon shows the increase in their photocatalytic properties [17].

The bandgap of the BaWO4 is evaluated by using the Tauc plot method and the bandgap is calculated by using equation (2) [23].
(2)
α h ν = A
( h ν -
E g
)
n
where α is the absorption coefficient, A is the bulk constant, the value of n is 2 for the direct bandgap and 1/2 for the indirect bandgap, h is the Planck constant, Eg optical bandgap energy, and c is the constant which depends upon the movement of electron-hole. The Tauc plots are shown in Fig. 9 .

The calculated optical band gap (Eg) of BaWO4 is 3.62 eV and for CQDs are 3.06 eV and the composites of BaWO4 doped with CQDs with various concentrations (B, C, D) which shows bandgaps 3.33 eV, 3.34 eV, and 2.93 eV which are less than the plain BaWO4 nanoparticles. This reduction in the bandgap of BaWO4 after doping with the CQDs can enhance their photocatalytic properties.

The Photoluminescence (PL) emission of A and (B, C, D) are given in Fig. 10 . This material belongs to a tetragonal type structure and they show scintillation property, i.e., they can convert the high-energy absorbed photons into the UV or visible photons [24]. The spectra were recorded by using the excitation radiation of 630 nm wavelength. The spectra of A (pure BaWO4) show two peaks at 653 nm and 677 nm and E (pure CQDs) show the spectra in the range of 630–1000 nm. When the CQDs are doped in the BaWO4 samples the emission spectra of BaWO4 change, showing a peak at 684.43 nm and it apparently is due to a shift from the 677 nm peak. The peak further shifts to 684.96 nm at higher CQD percentages. With CQD doping, the other PL peaks also have a redshift. The transition in this region is believed to come from the transition 5D0 → 7F2 (0–2) at high symmetric site occupancy of the CQDs.

The agar well diffusion method was utilized to investigate antibacterial properties by calculating the inhibition zone of the BaWO4 and BaWO4 doped with CQDs. Staphylococcus Gram-positive microbial strains were utilized to test the inhibition zones. Their solution (A, B, C, D) was made in the dimethyl sulfoxide. Their concentration was 200 mg/mL because this is the (MIC) minimum inhibitory concentration value. Using a cotton sterile swab, the lawn of test organisms was synthesized and after that, the discs containing the antimicrobial were put in the agar plate. The plates were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The inhibition zone is given by all 5 samples was calculated in the millimeter range and are listed in Table 2 . The antimicrobial properties were shown by the samples due to the surface oxygen species which can kill bacteria [4]. Due to the organic functional groups and amorphous surface introduced in the BaWO4 by doping of CQDs and an acidic condition, CQDs and the doped materials have higher potential to kill the bacteria. Ether and Carboxylic groups become protonated in a slightly acidic environment, the uneven concentration of carboxylic and ether on CQDs and CQDs doped BaWO4 are related to surface potential values which could boost their antibacterial activity. The inhibition zones are shown in Fig. 11 .

The zones increase as the value of CQDs increases but in D the 8 mL CQDs doped BaWO4 gives the small inhibition zone due to the agglomeration occurs as shown in Fig 10 (D). The zones increase because the functional groups on the surface of the CQDs/BaWO4 are increase and protonation occurs more which can kill the cell wall of the bacteria.

The antibacterial activity of synthesized composite (A, B, C, D, and E) was evaluated against bacterial strains S. aureus. The following formulae were used to calculate antibacterial activity and percentage decrease based on the number of surviving bacterial colonies for samples (A & C) at 0 h and 24 h.
(3)
%
A g e
R e d u c t i o n = [ ( l o g C t - l o g T t ) / l o g C t ] ∗ 100
(4)
A = F - G

Fig. 13 shows the obtained results, which are represented as logarithms of cfu/ml. the growth of bacterial colonies and the number of bacterial colonies that survived after 24 h as log numbers for all tested samples are depicted in this figure. It was evident from the graph data that sample A (pure BaWO4) has a little antibacterial activity while there is a significant decrease in the number of bacterial colonies for B and C samples. It is confirmed from the results that samples B (3 mL CQDs) and C (6 mL) have 87 % and 91 % antibacterial activity against S. aureus bacteria. This might be attributed due to the complete doping of carbon quantum dots with the barium tungstate. However, with the increase in the concentration of CQDs the bacterial activity decreases for sample D which might be due to the agglomeration of composite. Additionally, the CQDs showed antibacterial activity of about 72 %. The images in Fig. 12
depict the number of inoculated and surviving bacterial colonies at 0 h and 24 h for A and C samples, respectively.
Thus, the synthesized composite exhibits excellent antibacterial activity against the S. aureus bacterial strain.

The CQDs-doped barium tungstate was synthesized by the hydrothermal route at 170 °C for 24 hr. The as-prepared composite was in the nanometer range. The XRD results confirm the as-synthesized nanocomposite has a tetragonal scheelite structure having crystal sizes ranging from 31 nm to 34 nm. The SEM results verify the shape of the as-synthesized CQDs doped barium tungstate was like a nano-sphere. The TEM results confirm the presence of CQDs in BaWO4. The EDS result verifies the elements present in the CQDs doped barium tungstate by weight percent and is consistent with the design of the experiment. The UV results explain the absorption maxima of CQDs and BaWO4 and their nanocomposites with various concentrations of the CQDs. The bandgap of all samples calculated by the Tauc plots verifies the reducing of bandgap by increasing concentrations of CQDs. The PL spectra show the shifting of emission spectra due to the doping of CQDs showing the increase in the surface oxygen species. Antibacterial properties were investigated by the agar well diffusion method and their inhibition zones were calculated. The inhibition zone of BaWO4 doped with CQDs is larger than the bare CQDs because of the introduction of the protonated functional groups like ether and carboxylic groups in CQDs/BaWO4. The quantitative analysis confirms the antibacterial activity increases as the concentration of CQDs increases.

Nouman Aslam: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Saba Akram: Formal analysis, Investigation. M.I. Yousaf: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Abdulrahman Alshammari: Funding acquisition. Norah A. Albekairi: Funding acquisition. D.J. Fu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to Researchers Supporting Project # (RSP2024R491), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia
